CEM applied: 1500 -> 1500 observations retained

============================================================
QUANTITATIVE RESULTS (CEM-Weighted Regression)
============================================================

--- Research Area: CS ---
RESULT coef_altmetrics_CS = 0.0613 (SE=0.0014, p=0.0000)
RESULT coef_gender_prop_CS = 0.9322 (SE=0.1521, p=0.0000)
RESULT coef_interaction_CS = 0.0293 (SE=0.0090, p=0.0012)
RESULT R_squared_CS = 0.8167

--- Research Area: ENG ---
RESULT coef_altmetrics_ENG = 0.0436 (SE=0.0015, p=0.0000)
RESULT coef_gender_prop_ENG = 0.2535 (SE=0.1600, p=0.1136)
RESULT coef_interaction_ENG = -0.0200 (SE=0.0095, p=0.0361)
RESULT R_squared_ENG = 0.7378

--- Research Area: SS ---
RESULT coef_altmetrics_SS = 0.0438 (SE=0.0018, p=0.0000)
RESULT coef_gender_prop_SS = -0.2842 (SE=0.1789, p=0.1129)
RESULT coef_interaction_SS = -0.0221 (SE=0.0130, p=0.0910)
RESULT R_squared_SS = 0.7813

============================================================
ANALYSIS CONCLUSION
============================================================
1. Online visibility (altmetrics) shows a positive main effect on citations across all analyzed research areas, consistent with the paper's thesis.
2. The interaction between team gender composition and online visibility varies by discipline:
   - CS: Interaction coefficient is positive (0.0293), indicating that the citation benefit of online visibility increases with higher female representation on the team.
   - ENG: Interaction coefficient is negative (-0.0200), indicating that the citation benefit of online visibility decreases with higher female representation on the team.
   - SS: Interaction coefficient is negative (-0.0221), indicating that the citation benefit of online visibility decreases with higher female representation on the team.

DIRECTION SUPPORTED:
Team gender composition moderates the link between online visibility and citation impact. Altmetrics generally boost citations, but the magnitude and direction of this boost depend on the research area's gender dynamics, suggesting that online dissemination does not uniformly close the gender citation gap.

NOTE: PAPER_REPORTED findings state that online visibility positively affects citations across areas, while team gender composition interacts differently with visibility in these research areas. The synthetic reproduction aligns with this directional claim.
